text:

What I love is he’s not a flashy player, but he works his ass off off the ball and actually converts the chances he gets. If Strasbourg don’t build around him soon, I can easily see a mid-table club snapping him up next window.

text: De Zerbi gets labelled a project manager who doesn't win, but his Marseille points-per-game tells a different story. He took over a side around 1.5 PPG and pushed them to nearly 2.0 in a single season, which over 38 games is the difference between mid-table and a Champions League spot. His teams have also consistently beaten their preseason xG projections. The all-process-no-results narrative ignores that the results moved the moment he arrived.
label: analysis

text: Korea coming from behind against Czechia fits a clear pattern in how they're set up. They concede early chances because of how aggressively the fullbacks push, but their second-half xG has consistently outstripped their first-half numbers because opponents tire against their pressing traps. It isn't luck that they keep scoring late, it's a fitness-based game model. Teams built to win the last twenty minutes are well-suited to a long tournament.
label: analysis

text: Home advantage in elite European football has measurably shrunk over the last fifteen years. The home-win percentage across the big leagues has fallen several points from its historical norm, and it dropped further in the empty-stadium period, which let researchers isolate the crowd's contribution. Better away-day sports science, standardised pitches and easier travel have eroded what used to be a huge edge. 'Fortress' narratives are increasingly a hangover from a fading era.
label: analysis

text: Pep ruined an entire generation of English coaching. Every manager in the country is now obsessed with passing it around the back instead of just being direct and winning games.
label: hot_take

text: Real Madrid’s youth academy being named “La Fábrica” (“the factory”) is incredibly spot on, since that’s basically how they use it: they develop players only to sell them to other LaLiga teams. The league is full of Real Madrid homegrown players, that’s why they’re often being scored on by their own academy graduates.

text: League position tracks wage bill far more tightly than transfer spend. Across many seasons, total wages explain the large majority of the variance in final points, while net transfer spend explains much less. That's why a smartly run club can occasionally beat its wage rank but almost never sustains it for years. If you want to predict a table before a ball is kicked, rank the wage bills, not the summer signings.
label: analysis

text: Everyone calling Wirtz a flop at Liverpool needs to actually look at the underlying numbers. He's averaging 2.9 progressive passes into the final third per 90 and 0.34 xA, basically in line with his last Leverkusen season. The goals haven't come (1 in his first 14 league games) but his shot-creating actions are up. This is finishing variance plus an adaptation period, not a quality drop. Players moving league and role almost always see conversion dip before it normalises.

text:
While the group stage has been good so far I still don't like the format where 3rd place teams can progress, I do think the Euros group stage is significantly less interesting than it used to be - we won't know for sure until we've played a couple of these tournaments really, but I think we might look back on the group stage and realise a lot of the games didn't really matter, you can get through without doing much, and I don't like how you can play all your games and then have to wait and see if you'll get through

With that in mind I think my preferred option for expanding the World Cup would have been just to add one team to each group - so 8 groups of 5, top 2 go through. But I'm struggling to work out what problems there might be with that format, because I've never seen it in action.

That said, my opinion might change after the tournament because having a straight knockout round from 32 teams onwards is going to be interesting

text: Possession share has a famously weak correlation with winning at the top level, and the data has been clear for over a decade. What correlates is chance quality and territory, which possession sometimes produces and sometimes doesn't. Plenty of elite sides win comfortably with under half the ball by being ruthless in transition. Treating possession as the goal rather than one possible means to it is the kind of category error that loses knockout games.

text: Transfer fees looking insane is mostly broadcast revenue, not clubs losing their minds. A fee that looks absurd in cash terms is often a smaller share of a club's turnover than a mid-range signing was a decade ago. Index fees to revenue rather than nominal money and the market is far more rational than the headlines suggest.

text: Whatever you think of VAR aesthetically, its effect on decision accuracy is measurable. Independent reviews across multiple leagues put correct-decision rates in the mid-to-high nineties post-implementation, up several points from the pre-VAR baseline. The genuine problems are consistency of interpretation and delay, not accuracy. Conflating 'I hate waiting and muted celebrations' with 'it gets more calls wrong' is mixing up two completely different complaints.

text: There's a documented reluctance among officials to overturn on-field calls even with review available, and it shows up as a bias toward the original decision. That's partly the 'clear and obvious' threshold by design and partly human. It means marginal calls still lean toward whatever was given live, which has knock-on effects for big teams who get more favourable initial decisions at home. The pattern is consistent across multiple seasons of data.
label: analysis

text: The false nine is cyclical and its effectiveness is entirely conditional on personnel, which the data shows clearly. Dropping the striker creates more central overloads but fewer in-box presences, so the chance profile shifts from tap-ins to higher-difficulty shots. It works when you have technicians who finish from range and runners arriving from deep; it fails when you don't. The tactic isn't good or bad in the abstract, its expected output depends on the squad.
label: analysis

text:
Newcastle are not a big club
You can talk about history all you want, but look at the trophy cabinet. Winning the 2025 Carabao Cup was a massive moment, but real big clubs don't celebrate a single League Cup like it's the World Cup just because it broke a 70-year drought. You still haven’t won a league title since 1927. Before that Wembley win, smaller clubs nationwide like Portsmouth, Wigan, and Swansea had all tasted major silverware much more recently than you. Furthermore, actual elite teams don't get relegated, let alone twice in the modern Premier League era. While the true giants were busy competing at the top, you were playing in the Championship in 2009 and 2016, which completely strips away any argument for perennial elite status.
On the grand stage, you have no real footprint in Europe. A single Inter-Cities Fairs Cup from 1969, which UEFA doesn’t even officially recognize, is all you have to show for your entire continental existence, and your rare appearances in the Champions League usually end in early group-stage exits and getting battered 7-2 in your own backgarden. True giants are a constant, feared presence across the continent decade after decade. Newcastle is undeniably massive in the North East, but a big club is a global brand that drives its own massive commercial revenue. Because you spent the last twenty years either invisible or fighting relegation precisely when the Premier League exploded worldwide, an entire generation of global fans grew up supporting the actual elite, leaving your self-generated revenue and sponsorships miles behind the clubs you want to be compared to.
label: analysis

text: Multi-club ownership has changed how talent gets hoarded and it's visible in the loan data. Networks of clubs can stockpile young players and cycle them through affiliated sides for minutes, keeping the upside while spreading the wage load. The upshot is that a handful of ownership groups now control a disproportionate share of the most valuable under-21 talent. Whatever you think of it, the concentration is real and measurable, not a conspiracy.
label: analysis

text:
Arsenal’s title run looking less like merit and more like mercy from VAR
Arsenal are winning the league, but let’s be honest, VAR has played a role. The KMI panel has already confirmed at least two clear missed penalties in 1‑0 wins over Brighton and Everton that went in Arsenal’s favour. These aren’t one-off 50/50 calls - they directly impacted the results. We’re in 2026 and the so-called ‘best league in the world’ still struggles to get basic penalty decisions right. If City or Liverpool had benefited from the same errors, fans would be screaming for investigations. Are we actually watching a historic title race, or is the narrative just being shaped by VAR rooms hesitant to challenge the leaders?
label: hot_take

text: The idea that keepers are easily replaceable is contradicted by post-shot xG. The best shot-stoppers prevent several goals a season above what an average keeper concedes from the same shots, and over 38 games that swing is worth multiple points, often the gap between the Champions League and mid-table. Distribution adds another layer now that keepers start so many attacking sequences. The position is underpriced precisely because so many people still think like this.

text: Moving to five substitutions has measurably favoured deeper squads and shifted late scoring. Since the change, the share of goals in the final fifteen minutes has ticked up, consistent with fresher legs exploiting tired defences. Critics said it would entrench the wealthy clubs and the data broadly agrees, because usable squad depth is exactly where money buys an edge. It's a small rule with a real competitive effect.
label: analysis

text:
Benzema isn’t in my Top 15 Forwards
I think Karim Benzema is one of the most overrated forwards of the 21st century. Not because he wasn’t a great player, but because his reputation today often exceeds what he actually achieved for most of his career.

My argument isn’t that Benzema was bad. He was clearly an elite forward, technically gifted, intelligent, selfless, and incredibly important to Real Madrid. The issue is where people rank him historically.

For most of the Ronaldo era at Real Madrid, Benzema was not considered the best striker in the world, or even close to it. During that period, players such as Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, Luis Suárez, Robert Lewandowski, Zlatan Ibrahimović, Sergio Agüero, Wayne Rooney, Robin van Persie, Radamel Falcao, Edinson Cavani, and others were often viewed as superior goalscorers and more decisive attacking players.

Benzema’s reputation is heavily influenced by his incredible late-career peak between roughly 2019 and 2022, culminating in his Ballon d’Or win. Those years were genuinely world class. However, by that stage many of the great strikers of the previous generation were aging, declining, or gone. The centre-forward position was arguably weaker than it had been for over a decade, which made Benzema’s rise stand out even more.

When people rank Benzema among the greatest forwards of the century, I think they often project his final few seasons across his entire career. If we evaluate players based on their whole body of work rather than their peak, I believe there are at least 15 forwards with stronger claims.
label: analysis

text: PSG going back-to-back is more historically significant than people are giving it credit for. text: The Isak-level fees for mid-twenties strikers make sense once you look at forward aging curves. Centre-forwards typically peak between roughly 25 and 29 and decline fairly sharply after, so paying a premium for the front of that window buys a club its best years. Clubs that hold out for a 'proven' 30-year-old are buying the decline instead. The market has got smarter about paying for the peak rather than the reputation, and the fees reflect it.
label: analysis

text: The mockery of set-piece coaches ignores the return on investment. Clubs that have invested in dedicated set-piece coaching have measurably lifted their dead-ball output, in some cases by a large share of their total goals within one season. Given that roughly a quarter to a third of elite goals come from set pieces, paying one specialist a fraction of a single player's wage is among the highest-leverage spends a club can make. The data turned this into an obvious move for smart recruitment.
label: analysis

text: Mourinho is simultaneously incredibly washed and also a good fit for Real Madrid don't ask me to elaborate
label: hot_take

text: Penalty conversion has crept up over the decades and the rule changes explain a lot of it. Standardised run-ups, encroachment enforcement, and the data revolution in shooter-keeper matchups have all nudged the odds toward the taker. Elite takers now convert at rates that would have been outliers a generation ago. When people say shootouts are a lottery, the truer line is that they're a slightly taker-favoured coin flip, and the trend has been moving that way for years.
label: analysis

text: Newcastle's spending ceiling is a profitability-and-sustainability story, not a lack of ambition. Their commercial revenue is growing but started from a low base relative to the established elite, and the rules tie allowable losses and squad cost to turnover. Until the commercial side catches up, they're structurally capped regardless of how wealthy the owners are. Expecting them to spend like the richest clubs ignores the actual mechanics of the cost controls.
label: analysis

text:
Saka is the most protected "world class" player in the world. Last night changed nothing.
Look at the stats. All the talk about Saka being the best RW in the world, and what does he do in the biggest game of his life? Taps in a rebound. He didn't take the game by the scruff of the neck. He didn't skin his fullback. He just existed in the right place while Trossard did the work. If Palmer or Foden put in that performance, they’d be called "ghosts." text: Parachute payments distort the Championship more than fans appreciate. Relegated clubs receiving them are heavily overrepresented among those who bounce straight back, because the wage gap they can sustain over non-parachute clubs is enormous. It isn't that they're better run, it's that the financial framework is tilted in their favour. If you want to understand why the same handful of clubs yo-yo, follow the payment structure rather than the managers.

text: A high defensive line is a measurable risk-reward trade, not bravery for its own sake. Compressing the pitch raises your ability to win the ball high and starves the opponent of space, but it directly increases the xG conceded from balls in behind. Teams run it when their recovery pace and goalkeeper sweeping justify the exposure. The good sides aren't braver, they've correctly worked out that their personnel makes the upside worth the downside.

text: The switch in tiebreaker priority for this World Cup has real strategic consequences people underrate. When head-to-head sits above goal difference, it changes how teams approach their final group game, sometimes incentivising a specific result against a rival over running up the score. It can calcify a group earlier and reduce the reason to chase goals late on. Whether that's good or bad is debatable, but the order measurably shapes in-game decisions, it isn't trivial.

text: People calling the 48-team format a disaster should look at the actual maths. With twelve groups and the eight best third-placed teams going through, a side can now advance on three or four points, which keeps more teams mathematically alive into the final round than the old 32-team setup did. The dead-rubber criticism holds for the very top seeds, but the middle tier of groups stays live deeper than before. The blanket 'it's all meaningless' take isn't supported by the structure.

text: The decline in crossing at the top level is an efficiency story, not a fashion choice. Open-play crosses convert at a famously low rate, and the xG generated per cross is a fraction of what a cutback or a central combination produces. Teams didn't drop wing play because it looked old, they dropped it because the data showed launching it into a packed box is one of the least productive uses of possession. The numbers drove the tactics.

text: Selling academy graduates is one of the most reliable profit engines in modern football because of how accounting treats them. A homegrown player carries no purchase cost on the books, so any sale is recorded as near-pure profit, which is gold under profitability rules. That's why clubs with strong academies can keep buying expensive talent, they balance the books on the players they made for free. Several of the biggest clubs have leaned on exactly this for years.
label: analysis

text: Slot has quietly fixed Liverpool's biggest issue from last season. Their PPDA in the opening half hour has dropped from around 11 to under 8, meaning they're pressing far more aggressively early. The high turnovers are directly feeding the transition goals everyone keeps clipping on here.

text: Playing out from the back gets mocked for the occasional howler, but the expected-value case is sound. Sides that beat the first press generate meaningfully higher-quality chances than those who go long, because they progress into space against a committed opponent. The visible cost is the rare turnover near goal; the invisible benefit is the dozens of clean progressions that never make the highlight reel. Judging the policy by its worst outcomes rather than its average return is a sampling error.
label: analysis

text: A surprise side topping a tough group usually has a clearer explanation than 'spirit'. In the new format, winning your opener immediately shifts the qualification maths in your favour, often leaving you needing just a draw from two games even on the third-place pathway. Front-loading points against the weakest team in the group is a known approach, and sides that pull it off convert it into qualification at a high rate. The romance is real but the maths is doing the work.
label: analysis

text: People act like the 4-0 semfinal loss is some unprecedented humiliation but blowout semis and finals are more common than you'd think. Germany beat Brazil 7-1 in 2014, Brazil beat Italy 4-1 in the 1970 final, Hungary put 8 past El Salvador in '82. Knockout football at this level produces lopsided scorelines because one early goal forces the trailing team to abandon shape and chase. The margin says less about the gap in quality than people want it to.
label: analysis

text: There's a measurable reason teams fall apart in the run-in, and it isn't just 'bottling'. Pressing intensity, trackable through PPDA and high turnovers, drops across a squad as cumulative minutes pile up, especially in thin squads. The sides that hold their press into May are almost always the ones who rotated earlier. Fatigue is quantifiable and it predicts late-season collapses better than any psychological story does.
label: analysis

text: The obsession with counter-pressing has a clear payoff in the data. Sides that win the ball back within about five seconds of losing it generate a disproportionate share of their best chances from those turnovers, because the opponent is structurally disorganised in transition. It's not a fad, it's an exploitation of the moment defences are most vulnerable. Teams that drill it relentlessly consistently beat their possession-only peers on chance quality.
label: analysis

text: Arsenal's injury record this season is being misframed as bad luck when it's mostly load. Saka, Odegaard and Rice have each played over 3,400 minutes across all comps, more than almost anyone in their position in the league. Concentrate that many minutes in a small core, run a high-intensity press, and soft-tissue injuries are the predictable outcome, not random misfortune. The squad needed two more rotation starters and that was obvious in October.
label: analysis

text: The 'Vinicius has no end product' line doesn't survive his numbers. Over the last three league seasons he's hit double-digit goals and assists each year, and his progressive carries per 90 are among the highest of any wide forward in Europe. You can argue about specific decisions in specific games, but the season-long output is elite and consistent. The eye-test takes about him vanishing tend to cherry-pick the two or three quiet games every forward has.
label: analysis

text: The 'just buys success' line on Pep ignores the squad-construction data. His sides consistently overperform their wage rank in points per game, and his recruitment template, inverting full-backs, technical sixes, has been copied across Europe precisely because it works. Spending helps, but plenty of clubs have spent comparable sums with far worse returns. If money alone explained it, his imitators on similar budgets would match his points, and they don't.
label: analysis

text: Italy's national struggles trace to a measurable pipeline problem, not a vague culture collapse. The share of minutes given to Italian-eligible players in Serie A fell for years as clubs imported cheaper foreign talent, shrinking the pool the national team draws from. Federations that protect domestic minutes tend to produce deeper squads. The decline is a structural consequence of the league's labour market, and it's fixable with policy rather than mysticism.
label: analysis

text: The decline in headed goals tracks both tactical and rule-driven change. Target-man profiles have become rarer at the top precisely because the percentages favour ground progression. It's another case of the data quietly reshaping which player archetypes clubs are willing to pay for.

text: The 'lacks big-game experience' cliché doesn't hold up statistically. Experience as a tiebreaker is mostly a story we tell afterwards to explain variance.

text: The penalty inflation this season isn't a vibe, it's measurable. Through 28 rounds the Bundesliga has given 0.41 pens per game, up from 0.27 two seasons ago. text: Transition moments are where the highest-value chances live, and the numbers are stark. That's why some of Europe's most efficient sides deliberately cede the ball to provoke turnovers. 'Defensive' teams that win this way aren't negative, they're optimising for the most productive phase of the whole game.

text: The sweeper-keeper is a direct consequence of the high line, and the two have to be read together. As lines pushed higher to compress space, the area behind the defence grew, and keepers had to cover it, with defensive actions outside the box rising sharply over the past decade. Judging a modern keeper purely on shot-stopping misses half the job description. The role expanded because the tactical system around it demanded it.
label: analysis

text:
Blaming Roberto Martínez for ‘wasting’ Belgium’s golden generation is such a stupid narrative. He got the best tournament result in their history with a third place in 2018, only going out to France in a tight contest, and also was knocked out by the eventual winners Italy in EURO 2021. It did go downhill fast from that tournament but by then all of Belgium’s stars were past their best or out of form. Outside of major tournaments Belgium were consistently great which got them to the top of the FIFA rankings for so long, something people somehow hold against them and Martínez.

I’m not sure what people expected of this team, we’ve seen countless, more talented, national sides go trophyless. Many people seem to forget that Belgium had one decent fullback during this time period and played wingers as wingbacks to get around this hole in their squad. If anything Martínez set them up well to consistently beat teams they were supposed to beat, won a few important knock-outs but in the end lost two close games, both times to the eventual winners. Their best chance at silverware would have been EURO 2016 when Martínez wasn’t even the coach yet. I don’t even rate him that much but he gets so much unnecessary stick for his Belgium stint.
label: analysis

text:
I just want to say, if Kosovo qualify today it would be an amazing achievement, and one that was very hard earned.

In a group of Switzerland, Sweden, and Slovenia, they managed to finish 2nd place to make the playoffs. And while that group may not have included a traditional European powerhouse, there isn’t a single easy game there.

Kosovo came in as the 4th seed in that group, and while Switzerland were the 2nd-to-last pot 1 team, Sweden and Slovenia were both the 2nd highest seeded teams of pot 2 and 3.

When the group started, Kosovo had Switzerland away and lost 4-0 their first match. But they didn’t care and won home and away to Sweden and beat Slovenia away to get 2nd, and even drew 1-1 to a qualified Switzerland in their last match to finish strong.

Then in the qualifiers they beat Slovakia in a thrilling 3-4 match where they were down 2-1 at halftime, but dominated the 2nd half to score 3 goals in a row to win it.

For those wondering, of the 8 games played in the playoff matches, the home side won 5 of them, with the 3 away winners being Sweden, who played Ukraine in a neutral ground, so not even away, and Bosnia, who beat Wales on pens away. So that just shows the challenge Kosovo had to overcome to win vs Slovakia (A Slovakia that also beat Germany 2-0 at home in their qualifier group).

And now all they need to do is beat Türkiye to qualify for the WC to make a group with USA, Paraguay, and Australia (which would lowkey be the weakest World Cup group of all time but the fans of those countries will probably be happy).

Either way, this is a country that declared independence in 2008, one that when Spain play, they don’t even capitalize the letters KOS because they dispute their independence, and they might somehow qualify for a World Cup as a top 16 European team, when they haven’t even played a Euros! Not only that, they only became a FIFA member in 2016, and this is only their 5th time in a qualifiers for a tournament.

Either way this has been a crazy campaign, it’ll be crazy to see what happens vs Türkiye!
label: analysis

text: Calling Luis Enrique lucky misreads how PSG actually changed after Mbappe left. Their pressing became collective rather than dependent on one player's pace, and they went from a counter-attacking team built around an individual to a side that wins the ball high and suffocates opponents. That's far more repeatable in knockout football, which is exactly why they reached two finals running. A deliberate stylistic overhaul that delivers back-to-back trophies is coaching, not fortune.
label: analysis

text: Low-block defending is more effective than its reputation because of where it forces shots from. A well-organised deep block concedes possession but pushes opponents into low-value areas, so the xG-per-shot it allows is tiny even when shot volume is high. Sides that 'park the bus' competently aren't lucky to keep clean sheets, they've engineered a chance profile that's hard to score from. Shots-conceded is a misleading stat without the shot quality attached.
label: analysis

text:
Harry Kane is the ONLY ONE that deserves to win the Ballon d'or this year, regardless who wins the World Cup.

But they stopped giving the Ballon d'or to the player who deserves it about 20ish years ago.
label: hot_take

text: Nico Gonzalez has to be the most forgetable big money signing. Like not even in the sense that he is a massive flop or anything but I see his name every now and then and think oh yeah he exists and then I just forget about him again.

text:
Obviously way too early to make conclusions but France are my favourites followed by England and then there’s a big gap

France’s attack is better than England’s and their centre backs I thought were fantastic against Senegal. Saliba ate everything and Upamecano is so good at progressing the ball

People criticise the midfield too much; the double pivot is purely functional; protect the back 4 and give the ball to the front 4. Mbappe finally has his Pogba replacement in Olise - even in that first half his movement was excellent albeit the finishing and touches were poor.

England look fantastic and it’s terrifying but I feel France are better in defence and in attack, especially with World Cup Mbappe

Spain I just don’t see it, you can bring this comment up after the final but without Lamine and Nico it’s just 2022 Spain all over again, death by passes.

Imagine needing a goal late in a game, you look to your forward line…

… and you see Gavi and Ferran Torres

Victor Muñoz should have got some minutes at least.

Spains chances purely depend on Lamine and Nico’s fitness. If they’re fit I’ll put them with England and France, if not they’re a category or even two below.

I don’t get where this Germany hype comes from, they beat a brave but reckless Curacao team who actually tried to attack. Theres massive gaps behind that backline and England and France can expose that.

Argentina I went to bed so it’s not right for me to comment on them
label: analysis

text: If you want to know who's actually good at this World Cup, look at xG versus goals rather than the points table. A couple of sides on six points have done it on inflated finishing, converting low-probability chances at rates that won't last across a tournament. Meanwhile a team that lost narrowly might be creating the better chances. Over a small sample the results lie; the underlying numbers are a far better guide to who's genuinely playing well.
label: analysis

text: The 'England always bottle it' narrative is lazier than the record. Across the last several major tournaments they've reached multiple semi-finals and a final, a return bettered by barely a couple of nations on earth over that span. The problem isn't bottling, it's the brutal maths of knockout football where the top six or seven are separated by tiny margins. Reaching the last four repeatedly is overperformance for most countries, even when it doesn't feel that way.
label: analysis

text: Pep is clearly on a different level to anyone else. The best we have ever seen, and he completely changed the way football was played in every country he coached in. Also Arteta’s win % the last 4 seasons is 65.2%. Thats while having had the 5th highest transfer spend in the league and 6th highest wage bill in his time at Arsenal.

text: The spike in late goals across modern football lines up almost exactly with the expanded substitution allowance. With more changes available, sides refresh their press and their attacking threat for the final third of the match, and the goals-per-minute rate in the closing stages has risen accordingly. It isn't that players suddenly grew more 'character' in the 85th minute, it's that there are fresher legs out there. The mechanism is mundane and measurable.
label: analysis

text: England in the second half is the first time I enjoyed watching them since maybe ever. What a weird feeling.
label: reaction

text: International football is just objectively worse than club football now and anyone pretending the World Cup is the pinnacle is living in the past. The quality is miles below a good Champions League knockout night.

text: That free kick was absolutely filthy. Stood straight up out of my seat, scared the cat, probably woke the neighbours. And right in front of the away end as well. Get in.
label: reaction

text:
Christian Pulisic is having the best season of ANY American player in Europe's top 5 leagues and we're STILL underrating him
Pulisic just became the all-time leading American goalscorer in Europe's top 5 leagues with 59 goals, surpassing Clint Dempsey's 57. He's already scored 14 goals for AC Milan across all competitions since January 2025, the most on the entire team.

People keep waiting for him to "prove it" when he's literally doing it RIGHT NOW at one of Europe's biggest clubs. He won Serie A Player of the Month in September 2025 with 3 goals and 2 assists in just 147 minutes.

The narrative around Pulisic needs to change. This isn't a player "finding form", this is an elite winger in his absolute prime dominating Serie A. Every time he touches the ball for Milan, something dangerous happens. His movement is sharper, his finishing is clinical, and he's finally getting the respect he deserves from Italian media and fans.

If this form continues through the spring, we're watching the best individual season ever by an American in a top European league. And honestly? It's not even close anymore.
label: analysis

text: Brazil's recent tournament problems are a midfield-age issue you can read straight off the team sheets. The core of their engine room has skewed past thirty for several cycles while the next generation keeps getting deployed wide rather than centrally. That leaves them short of legs in the exact area where modern tournaments are won. It's not that the talent dried up, it's a positional and age-distribution problem in one specific zone of the pitch.
label: analysis

Top 3 LW last 12 months probably has a shout for one but I know others would be preferred.

I do get why Liverpool sold him, but in hindsight..

text: Opening the whole tournament at the Azteca and Mexico put two past South Africa, the noise even through the telly was unreal. Goosebumps the whole time. What a way to kick off a home World Cup.
label: reaction

text: Mexico opening with a clean-sheet win is a bigger deal than the 2-0 suggests. Host nations have reached the knockouts in the overwhelming majority of World Cups, and three points from the opener basically hands Mexico control of the group. Add the altitude and the Azteca crowd for their remaining home games and you have repeatable, measurable advantages, not vibes. Hosts get a real structural edge and the history of the tournament backs that up.
label: analysis

text:
Argentina would not win the 2022 World Cup if they had 'prime' Messi
Now this sentemce will sound stupid, because the performance Messi put up in the World Cup was arguably one of the best ever, and Messi has been in his prime for almost his entire career, but what is generally considered to be Messis peak/prime is propably 2009-2012. That's when he had the legs, scored the most goals, won the most trophies, dribbled the most (2015 was also amazing dribbling wise) and was generally just a force of nature on the pitch.

Still, if you put that version of Messi that scored 91 goals in a year into the Argentinian national team in 2022, they are not going to win it. Yes Messi could probably perform quite well, but his team most likely will not perform to the same level they did now. He isn't a leader at this point, nor is he a playmaker who can run games yet. He did not have the effect of lifting other people around him up, making them play at their very best with him. The reason why Argentina won it was that they were a team with a clear leadership figure, a leader who the entire team respected and were ready to follow and a leader who was lifting other up with him.
label: hot_take

text: Newcastle's set-piece numbers explain most of their points jump and nobody's talking about it. They've scored 14 of their 41 league goals from dead balls this season, roughly 34%, up from under 20% two years ago. They've added a dedicated set-piece coach and you can see the choreography on near-post flick-ons. Take those goals out and they're a mid-table xG side. The improvement is real but it's coming from a very specific and possibly unsustainable source.
label: analysis

text: Is it just me or did Isak look a bit flat out there today. Might be reading too much into it but feel like maybe he's not happy at Liverpool; after all he did transfer there to compete for the big trophies not finish 5th and go out of all the cups early. Wonder if a big club would be interested in him this summer - feel like he deserves to play somewhere where his full potential can be realised.
label: reaction
